title: Cloud Service & Deployment Models, Characteristics, and the Shared Responsibility Model
summary: Covers IaaS/PaaS/SaaS, public/private/community/hybrid deployment, government cloud and FedRAMP, the core defining characteristics of cloud, and how security duties are split between provider and customer.
content:
Cloud computing delivers on-demand IT resources (compute, storage, networking, software) over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis, instead of owning and maintaining physical hardware. NIST defines it by five essential characteristics: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.

Service models describe how much of the stack you manage versus the provider:
• IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — you rent raw virtual machines, storage, and networks (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VMs). You manage OS, runtime, and apps; provider manages physical hardware and virtualization.
• PaaS (Platform as a Service) — provider manages OS, runtime, and middleware; you deploy only your code/data (e.g., App Service, App Engine).
• SaaS (Software as a Service) — provider manages everything; you just use the application (e.g., Office 365, Gmail). A useful analogy: pizza-as-a-service — IaaS is take-and-bake, PaaS is delivery, SaaS is dining out.

Deployment models describe who can use the cloud and where it lives:
• Public cloud — shared infrastructure operated by a provider for many tenants.
• Private cloud — dedicated to a single organization (on-prem or hosted), favored for sensitive workloads.
• Community cloud — shared by several organizations with common requirements (e.g., agencies with the same compliance mandates).
• Hybrid cloud — combines public and private with orchestration between them, common in government for keeping classified/sensitive data private while bursting non-sensitive workloads to public cloud.

For US government and embassy contexts, providers offer dedicated 'government cloud' regions (AWS GovCloud, Azure Government, Google Assured Workloads) physically and logically isolated and staffed by screened US persons. FedRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) is the standardized program for assessing and authorizing cloud services for federal use; products earn an Authorization to Operate (ATO) at Low, Moderate, or High impact baselines. For Department of Defense and some State Department workloads, the DoD Cloud Computing Security Requirements Guide (SRG) defines Impact Levels (IL2, IL4, IL5, IL6 for classified).

The shared responsibility model is the single most tested security concept: the provider secures the cloud (physical facilities, hardware, hypervisor, managed-service infrastructure), while the customer secures what they put in the cloud (data, identities, access configuration, OS patching where applicable). Responsibility shifts with the service model — under IaaS the customer patches the OS; under SaaS the provider handles nearly everything except data classification and access management. The customer is always responsible for their own data and who can access it.

Core operational characteristics worth distinguishing: on-demand self-service means provisioning without human intervention; elasticity means automatically scaling resources up and down with demand; multi-tenancy means multiple customers share pooled physical resources while being logically isolated. Scaling comes in two forms — vertical scaling (scale up) means adding more power (CPU/RAM) to a single instance and usually requires a restart and has a hardware ceiling; horizontal scaling (scale out) means adding more instances behind a load balancer, which is more resilient and the cloud-native default.
keyPoints: NIST's five essential characteristics: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
IaaS = you manage OS/runtime/app; PaaS = you manage app/data only; SaaS = you just use the software.
Deployment models: public (shared), private (single org), community (shared by similar orgs), hybrid (public + private orchestrated).
FedRAMP standardizes security authorization for federal cloud use, granting an ATO at Low/Moderate/High baselines; DoD adds Impact Levels via the SRG.
Government cloud regions (AWS GovCloud, Azure Government) are isolated and operated by screened US persons for sensitive workloads.
Shared responsibility: provider secures 'the cloud' (hardware/hypervisor); customer secures what's 'in the cloud' (data, identity, access).
Responsibility shifts toward the provider as you move from IaaS to PaaS to SaaS, but the customer always owns data and access control.
Vertical scaling = bigger single machine (scale up, has a ceiling); horizontal scaling = more machines behind a load balancer (scale out).
Multi-tenancy means logically isolated customers sharing pooled physical infrastructure.
Elasticity (automatic scale up/down with demand) differs from raw scalability (the ability to grow at all).

flashcards.front: What are the three main cloud service models?
flashcards.back: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service), PaaS (Platform as a Service), and SaaS (Software as a Service).

flashcards.front: Under IaaS, who patches the guest operating system?
flashcards.back: The customer. IaaS gives you raw VMs/storage/network; the provider only secures the underlying hardware and hypervisor.

flashcards.front: Under SaaS, what does the customer still remain responsible for?
flashcards.back: Their own data and access management (who can log in / data classification). The provider handles essentially everything else.

flashcards.front: Define the shared responsibility model.
flashcards.back: The provider is responsible for security OF the cloud (physical, hardware, hypervisor, managed-service infra); the customer is responsible for security IN the cloud (data, identities, access config, OS where applicable).

flashcards.front: What is FedRAMP?
flashcards.back: The Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program — a standardized US government program for security assessment, authorization, and continuous monitoring of cloud products, granting an Authorization to Operate (ATO).

flashcards.front: What are the three FedRAMP impact baselines?
flashcards.back: Low, Moderate, and High — based on the impact of a confidentiality/integrity/availability breach (FIPS 199).

flashcards.front: What is a hybrid cloud and why is it common in government?
flashcards.back: A combination of private and public cloud with orchestration between them; government uses it to keep sensitive/classified data in a private cloud while bursting non-sensitive workloads to public cloud.

flashcards.front: Difference between vertical and horizontal scaling?
flashcards.back: Vertical (scale up) = add CPU/RAM to one instance (has a ceiling, often needs restart). Horizontal (scale out) = add more instances behind a load balancer (more resilient, cloud-native).

flashcards.front: What is multi-tenancy?
flashcards.back: Multiple customers (tenants) sharing the same pooled physical infrastructure while remaining logically isolated from each other.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between elasticity and on-demand self-service?
flashcards.back: Elasticity = resources automatically scale up and down with demand. On-demand self-service = a user can provision resources without human intervention from the provider.

flashcards.front: Name a US government cloud region offering and what makes it special.
flashcards.back: AWS GovCloud (US) or Azure Government — physically and logically isolated regions operated by screened US persons to meet federal compliance like FedRAMP High and ITAR.

flashcards.front: What is a community cloud?
flashcards.back: Cloud infrastructure shared by several organizations with common concerns (e.g., the same compliance/mission requirements), such as a group of federal agencies.

flashcards.front: In the 'pizza as a service' analogy, what is SaaS?
flashcards.back: Dining out — everything is provided; you just consume the application. (IaaS = take-and-bake, PaaS = delivery.)

flashcards.front: What is measured service?
flashcards.back: Cloud resource usage is metered/monitored, enabling pay-as-you-go billing and transparency for both provider and customer.

title: Cloud Storage, Networking, and Compute Fundamentals
summary: Covers object/block/file storage and tiers, durability vs availability, VPC networking (subnets, security groups, NACLs, NAT, peering, load balancing, CDN, DNS), and compute including VMs, containers, Kubernetes, and serverless.
content:
Cloud storage comes in three fundamental types:
• Object storage (e.g., Amazon S3, Azure Blob) stores data as objects with metadata in a flat namespace accessed via API/HTTP; ideal for unstructured data, backups, media, and static websites — massively scalable but not a filesystem.
• Block storage (e.g., EBS, Azure Managed Disks) provides raw volumes attached to a single VM like a hard drive; low-latency, used for databases and OS disks.
• File storage (e.g., EFS, Azure Files) provides a shared filesystem (NFS/SMB) multiple machines can mount simultaneously.

Storage tiers trade cost against access speed and retrieval fees: hot tier for frequently accessed data (highest storage cost, cheapest/instant access); cool tier for infrequently accessed data (lower storage cost, retrieval fees); cold/archive tier (e.g., S3 Glacier) for rarely accessed long-term retention (cheapest storage, but retrieval can take minutes to hours and costs more). Government records-retention requirements make archive tiers attractive for compliance copies.

Durability vs availability are distinct and commonly confused. Durability is the probability your data is NOT lost over time (e.g., S3's '11 nines' = 99.999999999% durability — designed to lose virtually nothing). Availability is the probability the service is reachable/up right now (e.g., 99.9% availability, the 'three nines' SLA). 'Nines' shorthand: 99.9% ≈ 8.76 hours downtime/year, 99.99% ≈ 52.6 minutes/year, 99.999% ≈ 5.26 minutes/year. Durability is achieved by replicating data across devices and zones; availability by redundancy and failover.

Networking centers on the Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) — your logically isolated private network in the cloud, divided into subnets. Public subnets route to the internet via an Internet Gateway; private subnets do not. A NAT gateway lets instances in private subnets make outbound internet connections (e.g., for updates) without being reachable inbound. Two firewall layers exist: security groups are stateful and operate at the instance level (return traffic is automatically allowed; allow-rules only); network ACLs (NACLs) are stateless and operate at the subnet level (you must allow both directions; support deny rules). VPC peering connects two VPCs privately so resources communicate as if on one network.

Traffic distribution and delivery: a load balancer spreads incoming requests across multiple healthy instances (Layer 4 network vs Layer 7 application load balancers) and performs health checks — the foundation of horizontal scaling and high availability. A CDN (Content Delivery Network, e.g., CloudFront) caches content at edge locations close to users to cut latency and offload origin servers — valuable for serving embassy/public-facing sites globally. Cloud DNS (e.g., Route 53) translates domain names to IP addresses and can route based on latency, geography, or health.

Compute options range from heavy to light:
• Virtual machines (VMs) emulate a full computer with its own guest OS on a hypervisor — strong isolation but heavier (each carries an OS).
• Containers package an app with its dependencies and share the host OS kernel — far lighter and faster to start than VMs, portable, and the basis of microservices (Docker is the common runtime).
• Kubernetes (K8s) is the dominant container orchestration platform: it schedules containers (grouped into pods) across a cluster of worker nodes managed by a control plane, handling scaling, self-healing, and rollouts.
• Serverless / FaaS (Functions as a Service, e.g., AWS Lambda) runs your code in response to events with no server management and pay-per-execution billing; it scales to zero. The main drawback is cold start — latency when a function spins up from idle because the runtime must be initialized.
keyPoints: Object storage = flat, API-accessed, unstructured/backups (S3); block storage = raw volume attached to one VM, low latency (databases); file storage = shared mountable filesystem (NFS/SMB).
Hot tier = frequent access, costly storage/cheap retrieval; cool = infrequent; cold/archive (Glacier) = cheapest storage but slow, costlier retrieval.
Durability = data won't be lost (S3 ~11 nines); availability = service is reachable now (e.g., 99.9% SLA). They are different metrics.
'Nines': 99.9% ≈ 8.8 hrs/yr downtime, 99.99% ≈ 53 min/yr, 99.999% ≈ 5 min/yr.
A VPC is your isolated cloud network; public subnets reach the internet via an Internet Gateway, private subnets use a NAT gateway for outbound-only access.
Security groups are stateful, instance-level, allow-only; NACLs are stateless, subnet-level, and support explicit deny rules.
VPC peering privately connects two VPCs; load balancers distribute traffic across healthy instances and enable horizontal scaling.
A CDN caches content at edge locations to reduce latency; cloud DNS resolves names to IPs and can route by latency/geo/health.
Containers share the host OS kernel and are lighter/faster than VMs, which each run a full guest OS.
Kubernetes orchestrates containers (pods) across a cluster; serverless/FaaS runs event-driven code with pay-per-use billing and can scale to zero but suffers cold-start latency.

flashcards.front: When would you use object storage vs block storage?
flashcards.back: Object storage (S3) for unstructured data, backups, media, static sites — flat namespace via API. Block storage (EBS) for low-latency volumes attached to a single VM, like databases and OS disks.

flashcards.front: What is file storage in the cloud?
flashcards.back: A shared filesystem (NFS/SMB, e.g., EFS/Azure Files) that multiple machines can mount and access simultaneously.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between durability and availability?
flashcards.back: Durability = the probability your data is not lost over time (e.g., S3's 11 nines). Availability = the probability the service is up and reachable right now (e.g., 99.9% SLA).

flashcards.front: What does '99.999%' (five nines) availability mean in downtime?
flashcards.back: About 5.26 minutes of downtime per year.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hot, cool, and cold/archive storage tiers?
flashcards.back: Hot = frequent access (high storage cost, cheap/instant retrieval). Cool = infrequent access (lower storage cost, retrieval fees). Cold/archive = rare access, long-term (cheapest storage, slow and costlier retrieval).

flashcards.front: What is a VPC?
flashcards.back: A Virtual Private Cloud — your logically isolated private network within the cloud provider, subdivided into subnets you control.

flashcards.front: What does a NAT gateway do?
flashcards.back: Lets instances in a private subnet initiate outbound internet connections (e.g., for patches) without allowing inbound connections from the internet.

flashcards.front: Security groups vs NACLs?
flashcards.back: Security groups: stateful, instance-level, allow-rules only (return traffic auto-allowed). NACLs: stateless, subnet-level, support both allow and deny rules (must allow both directions).

flashcards.front: What is VPC peering?
flashcards.back: A private connection between two VPCs that lets their resources communicate as if on the same network, without traversing the public internet.

flashcards.front: What does a CDN do?
flashcards.back: Caches content at edge locations close to users to reduce latency and offload origin servers (e.g., CloudFront).

flashcards.front: What is the role of a load balancer?
flashcards.back: It distributes incoming traffic across multiple healthy instances and runs health checks, enabling horizontal scaling and high availability (L4 network or L7 application).

flashcards.front: How do containers differ from virtual machines?
flashcards.back: VMs each run a full guest OS on a hypervisor (heavier, strong isolation). Containers share the host OS kernel, packaging only the app and dependencies (lighter, faster to start, portable).

flashcards.front: What is Kubernetes?
flashcards.back: A container orchestration platform that schedules containers (grouped as pods) across a cluster of worker nodes via a control plane, handling scaling, self-healing, and rollouts.

flashcards.front: What is serverless / FaaS and its main drawback?
flashcards.back: Functions as a Service (e.g., AWS Lambda) runs event-driven code with no server management and pay-per-execution billing; it can scale to zero. Main drawback: cold-start latency when a function initializes from idle.

flashcards.front: What is cloud DNS used for?
flashcards.back: Resolving domain names to IP addresses (e.g., Route 53), with the ability to route traffic based on latency, geography, or endpoint health.

title: Cloud Security, Reliability, Cost, and Operations
summary: Covers IAM and key/secrets management, WAF and MFA, disaster recovery (RTO/RPO, backups vs snapshots), cost models and egress, IaC, data residency/sovereignty, migration strategies, cloud bursting, and vendor lock-in.
content:
Identity and access management (IAM) is the backbone of cloud security. IAM defines who (users, groups, roles) can do what (permissions) to which resources, governed by policies — JSON documents granting or denying specific actions. Best practice is least privilege (grant only what's needed). An IAM role is an identity with permissions that can be temporarily assumed — preferred over long-lived access keys, so a VM or service gets short-lived credentials instead of hard-coded secrets. The root account should be locked down, used almost never, and protected with MFA (multi-factor authentication) — enabling MFA on root is a baseline control and a frequent exam point.

Protecting data and secrets: a KMS (Key Management Service) creates and controls encryption keys for encrypting data at rest and in transit, with centralized key rotation and audit logging. A secrets manager securely stores and rotates credentials, API keys, and database passwords so they never sit in code or config files. A WAF (Web Application Firewall) filters and blocks malicious HTTP traffic (SQL injection, cross-site scripting) at the application layer (Layer 7), protecting public-facing sites such as embassy portals.

Reliability and disaster recovery hinge on two metrics: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is how quickly you must restore service after an outage (acceptable downtime); RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is how much data loss is acceptable, measured backward in time (how often you must back up). A 1-hour RPO means backups at least hourly. Backups vs snapshots: a snapshot is a point-in-time copy of a specific volume/disk (often incremental, fast, same-platform), while a backup is typically a separate, retained, often cross-region/offsite copy intended for long-term recovery. Multi-AZ (availability zone) deployments protect against datacenter failure; multi-region against regional disasters.

Cost management is a core competency. Pricing models: on-demand (pay per use, no commitment, most flexible/expensive), reserved instances or savings plans (commit 1–3 years for a large discount, best for steady workloads), and spot instances (deeply discounted spare capacity that can be reclaimed with little notice — great for fault-tolerant batch jobs, risky for critical services). Egress costs are a notorious surprise: providers charge to move data OUT of the cloud (and across regions), while ingress is usually free — this fuels both budget overruns and vendor lock-in. Tagging (key-value labels on resources) enables cost allocation, chargeback to programs/bureaus, automation, and governance.

Operational and strategic concepts:
• Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — defining infrastructure in version-controlled declarative files (Terraform, CloudFormation, ARM/Bicep) so environments are repeatable, auditable, and reviewable rather than hand-clicked. Critical for consistency and compliance evidence.
• Data residency / sovereignty — laws requiring data to be physically stored and processed within specific borders and subject to that jurisdiction's laws. Highly relevant to government and embassies: choosing in-country or US-based regions to satisfy sovereignty and treaty obligations, and being aware that foreign-hosted data may be subject to foreign legal access.
• Migration — 'lift-and-shift' (rehosting) moves an application to the cloud with minimal changes: fast and low-risk but doesn't exploit cloud-native efficiencies; replatforming and refactoring offer more optimization at higher effort (part of the '6 Rs' of migration).
• Cloud bursting — a hybrid pattern where an application runs in a private cloud/datacenter and 'bursts' into public cloud only when demand spikes, balancing control and cost with elastic overflow capacity.
• Vendor lock-in — dependence on one provider's proprietary services making it costly/hard to migrate (due to egress fees, proprietary APIs, and retraining). Mitigations include open standards, containers/Kubernetes for portability, and multi-cloud or abstraction layers — a strategic concern for government procurement and avoiding single-supplier risk.
keyPoints: IAM controls who can do what to which resources via policies; follow least privilege and prefer temporary roles over long-lived keys.
Always enable MFA on the root account and avoid using root for daily operations.
KMS manages encryption keys; a secrets manager stores/rotates credentials so they're not hard-coded; a WAF blocks Layer-7 attacks like SQLi/XSS.
RTO = acceptable downtime to recover; RPO = acceptable data loss measured in time (drives backup frequency).
Snapshots are fast point-in-time volume copies (often same-platform/incremental); backups are separate retained, often offsite/cross-region copies.
Cost models: on-demand (flexible, priciest), reserved/savings plans (1–3 yr commit, big discount, steady loads), spot (cheapest, can be reclaimed, for fault-tolerant work).
Egress (data out) is charged while ingress is usually free; this drives surprise costs and reinforces vendor lock-in. Tagging enables cost allocation and governance.
Infrastructure as Code (Terraform, CloudFormation) makes environments repeatable, version-controlled, and auditable.
Data residency/sovereignty: laws requiring data stay within borders/jurisdiction — central to government and embassy region selection.

Lift-and-shift (rehost) is fast/low-risk migration with minimal change; cloud bursting overflows from private into public cloud on demand; vendor lock-in is mitigated by open standards and containers.

flashcards.front: What is an IAM policy?
flashcards.back: A document (usually JSON) that defines permissions — which actions are allowed or denied on which resources for a user, group, or role.

flashcards.front: Why prefer IAM roles over long-lived access keys?
flashcards.back: Roles provide temporary, automatically-rotated credentials that a service/VM assumes, avoiding hard-coded long-lived secrets that can leak.

flashcards.front: What is the principle of least privilege?
flashcards.back: Granting an identity only the minimum permissions required to do its job — nothing more.

flashcards.front: Why enable MFA on the root account?
flashcards.back: The root account has unrestricted power; MFA adds a second authentication factor so a stolen password alone can't compromise the entire environment. It's a baseline security control.

flashcards.front: What does a KMS do?
flashcards.back: A Key Management Service creates, stores, rotates, and controls access to encryption keys for protecting data at rest and in transit, with audit logging.

flashcards.front: What is a secrets manager for?
flashcards.back: Securely storing and automatically rotating credentials, API keys, and passwords so they aren't embedded in code or configuration files.

flashcards.front: What does a WAF protect against?
flashcards.back: A Web Application Firewall filters malicious HTTP/Layer-7 traffic such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS) against web apps.

flashcards.front: Difference between RTO and RPO?
flashcards.back: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how fast you must restore service (acceptable downtime). RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data loss is tolerable, measured in time (sets backup frequency).

flashcards.front: Difference between a snapshot and a backup?
flashcards.back: A snapshot is a fast point-in-time copy of a specific volume (often incremental, same-platform). A backup is a separate, retained, often cross-region/offsite copy for long-term recovery.

flashcards.front: Compare on-demand, reserved, and spot instances.
flashcards.back: On-demand: pay-per-use, no commitment, most expensive/flexible. Reserved/savings plans: 1–3 year commitment for big discounts, best for steady workloads. Spot: deeply discounted spare capacity that can be reclaimed anytime, for fault-tolerant jobs.

flashcards.front: What are egress costs?
flashcards.back: Charges for moving data OUT of the cloud (and often across regions). Ingress is usually free. Egress fees cause surprise bills and reinforce vendor lock-in.

flashcards.front: What is resource tagging used for?
flashcards.back: Key-value labels on resources that enable cost allocation/chargeback, automation, organization, and governance.

flashcards.front: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
flashcards.back: Defining and provisioning infrastructure through version-controlled declarative files (e.g., Terraform, CloudFormation) so environments are repeatable, auditable, and peer-reviewed.

flashcards.front: What is data residency / sovereignty?
flashcards.back: Legal requirements that data be physically stored/processed within a specific country or jurisdiction and be subject to its laws — key to government/embassy region selection.

flashcards.front: What is lift-and-shift migration?
flashcards.back: Rehosting an application to the cloud with minimal or no changes — fast and low-risk, but it doesn't take advantage of cloud-native optimizations.

flashcards.front: What is cloud bursting?
flashcards.back: A hybrid pattern where an app runs in a private cloud/datacenter and 'bursts' into public cloud for extra capacity only when demand spikes.

flashcards.front: What is vendor lock-in and how is it mitigated?
flashcards.back: Dependence on one provider's proprietary services that makes migration costly/difficult (egress fees, proprietary APIs). Mitigated via open standards, containers/Kubernetes for portability, and multi-cloud strategies.
